title: Le Chatelier's Principle: Concentration and Temperature
subtitle: Predicting which way an equilibrium will move
subject: Chemistry
syllabusDotPoints: investigate the effects of temperature, concentration, volume and/or pressure on a system at equilibrium and explain how Le Chatelier's principle can be used to predict such effects, for example: changing concentration; changing temperature
inquiryQuestion: What factors affect equilibrium and how?
lessonIntent: Students can apply Le Chatelier's principle to predict the direction of shift for concentration and temperature changes, and know that only temperature changes Keq.
examSkill: Give the direction of shift AND justify with LCP language; identify that concentration shifts position only while temperature changes Keq.

introVoiceover.text: Welcome to HSC Science. This is HSC Chemistry. Today, Le Chatelier's principle with concentration and temperature. Let's get into it.

scenes.caption: Le Chatelier's Principle: Concentration and Temperature (Year 12 Chemistry, Module 5, Lesson 5)

scenes.heading: A colour you can command
scenes.body: Add iron to a deep-red solution and it darkens. Le Chatelier predicted exactly this in 1884.
scenes.callout: Disturb an equilibrium and it pushes back.
scenes.caption: Adding Fe³⁺ to iron(III) thiocyanate deepens the red — Le Chatelier in action.

voiceover.text: OK, picture a test tube of deep red iron thiocyanate solution. Add a few drops of iron three nitrate, and the red gets visibly darker. Add something that pulls iron out of solution, and it fades. Back in 1884, Henri Le Chatelier used this exact reaction to demonstrate a principle that lets you predict the direction of any equilibrium change without a single calculation. The idea is simple, disturb a system at equilibrium, and it shifts to push back against your disturbance. Today we'll handle the two most common disturbances, changing concentration and changing temperature. And there's one critical distinction lurking in here, only one of those two actually changes the equilibrium constant. Let's find out which.

scenes.heading: Le Chatelier's principle
scenes.body: Disturb a system at equilibrium and it shifts to minimise, not eliminate, the disturbance.

bullets.text: A disturbance can be a concentration, temperature, or pressure change.
The system shifts in the direction that partially counteracts the change.
It only minimises the disturbance; it never fully cancels it.

scenes.secondary: A catalyst is NOT a disturbance, it speeds both directions equally. And LCP gives direction only, never the new concentrations; those need Keq and an ICE table later.
scenes.callout: The system pushes back, but only partway.
scenes.caption: LCP: a disturbed equilibrium shifts to partially counteract the disturbance.

voiceover.text: Let's state the principle properly. When a closed system at dynamic equilibrium is disturbed by a change in conditions, it shifts in the direction that minimises the effect of that disturbance and reaches a new equilibrium. The key word is minimise. The system partially counteracts your change, it does not eliminate it. So if you add more reactant, the system shifts forward and consumes some of what you added, but not all of it. The new equilibrium has more reactant and more product than before. Two quick boundaries. A catalyst is not a disturbance, because it speeds up both directions equally, so it doesn't cause a shift. And Le Chatelier's principle only tells you the direction of the shift, left or right, never the exact new concentrations. For numbers you'll need the equilibrium constant and an ICE table, which come later in the module.

scenes.heading: The words you need

bullets.text: Le Chatelier's principle: a disturbed equilibrium shifts to partially oppose the disturbance.
Concentration disturbance: adding or removing a reactant or product.
Position of equilibrium: a qualitative description of which side is favoured.
Equilibrium constant (Keq): the product-to-reactant ratio, changed ONLY by temperature.

scenes.callout: Position can shift without Keq ever changing.
scenes.caption: LCP, concentration disturbance, position of equilibrium, equilibrium constant.

voiceover.text: Four terms to lock in. Le Chatelier's principle, a system at equilibrium that's disturbed will shift to partially oppose the disturbance. A concentration disturbance just means adding or removing a reactant or product. The position of equilibrium is a qualitative description of which side is favoured, whether you've got mostly products or mostly reactants. And the equilibrium constant is the ratio of products to reactants at equilibrium, and here's the one to burn in, it is changed only by temperature. That last distinction runs through this entire lesson. The position of equilibrium can shift left or right all day long when you change concentrations, but the equilibrium constant itself only moves when the temperature changes.

scenes.heading: Temperature is the special one
scenes.body: Heating shifts an equilibrium in the endothermic direction and actually changes Keq.

bullets.text: Treat heat as a reactant or product: exothermic puts heat on the right.
Exothermic forward, heat it: shifts left, Keq decreases.
Endothermic forward, heat it: shifts right, Keq increases.

scenes.secondary: Concentration and pressure shift the position but leave Keq fixed. Temperature is the only disturbance that changes the value of Keq itself.
scenes.callout: Heat toward the endothermic side; only T moves Keq.
scenes.caption: Heating shifts toward the endothermic direction and changes Keq.

voiceover.text: Now the disturbance that behaves differently, temperature. A clean way to think about it, treat heat as if it were a reactant or a product. For an exothermic forward reaction, heat is released, so picture heat sitting on the product side. Now if you heat that system, it's like adding a product, so it shifts left, toward reactants, and crucially the equilibrium constant decreases. For an endothermic forward reaction, heat is absorbed, so heat sits on the reactant side, and heating shifts the equilibrium right, toward products, with the equilibrium constant increasing. The simple rule, heating always shifts the equilibrium in the endothermic direction, the direction that soaks up your added heat. And the big contrast with everything else, concentration and pressure shift only the position, but temperature is the one and only disturbance that changes the actual value of the equilibrium constant.

scenes.heading: Disturbing an esterification
scenes.question: CH₃COOH + C₂H₅OH ⇌ CH₃COOC₂H₅ + H₂O at equilibrium. Predict the shift for: (a) more acetic acid added; (b) ester removed by distillation; (c) water added.
scenes.coachNote: Add a species → shift away from it; remove a species → shift toward it.
steps: (a) Acetic acid is a reactant; adding it raises the forward rate → shift right, more ester.
(b) The ester is a product; removing it lowers the reverse rate → shift right to replace it.
(c) Water is a product here; adding it raises the reverse rate → shift left.
Keq is unchanged throughout, since temperature is constant.
scenes.caption: Esterification: add reactant → right; remove product → right; add product (water) → left.

voiceover.text: Let's work one with concentration changes. The esterification reaction, acetic acid plus ethanol giving ethyl acetate plus water, sits at equilibrium. Part a, you add more acetic acid. That's a reactant, so adding it raises the forward rate, and the system shifts right, making more ester. Part b, you remove the ester by distilling it off as it forms. The ester is a product, and removing it drops the reverse rate, so the system shifts right to replace some of it, a classic industrial trick to drive a reaction forward. Part c, you add water. Now here's a thing to watch, in this reaction water is a product, not just the solvent. So adding it raises the reverse rate and the system shifts left, back toward acid and ethanol. And through all three, the equilibrium constant never changes, because the temperature stayed constant. Concentration changes move the position, not the constant.

scenes.heading: Heating the Contact process
scenes.question: 2SO₂(g) + O₂(g) ⇌ 2SO₃(g), ΔH = −196 kJ mol⁻¹, at equilibrium at 450°C with Keq = 1.7 × 10⁵. Predict the shift and the change in Keq when (a) T rises to 600°C, (b) T falls to 300°C.
scenes.coachNote: Exothermic forward means heat behaves like a product.
steps: Forward is exothermic, so heat sits on the product side.
(a) Raising T to 600°C is like adding product → shift left, less SO₃.
Shift left means a smaller products/reactants ratio → Keq falls below 1.7 × 10⁵.
(b) Lowering T to 300°C → shift right, more SO₃ → Keq rises above 1.7 × 10⁵.
Answer: (a) left, Keq decreases; (b) right, Keq increases ✓
scenes.caption: Exothermic Contact process: heat → left (Keq down); cool → right (Keq up).

voiceover.text: Now a temperature example, the Contact process, two sulfur dioxide plus oxygen giving sulfur trioxide, exothermic with an enthalpy change of minus one hundred and ninety-six, at equilibrium at four hundred and fifty degrees with an equilibrium constant of one-point-seven times ten to the five. Part a, you raise the temperature to six hundred degrees. The forward reaction is exothermic, so heat behaves like a product. Adding heat is like adding product, so the system shifts left, making less sulfur trioxide. And because it shifted toward reactants, the products-over-reactants ratio shrinks, so the equilibrium constant drops below one-point-seven times ten to the five. Part b, you cool it to three hundred degrees. Now you're removing heat, the system shifts right, makes more sulfur trioxide, and the equilibrium constant climbs above its original value. Notice temperature did two things at once, it shifted the position and it changed the constant, which concentration changes never do.

scenes.heading: Three traps
scenes.body: Trap 1: thinking adding reactant increases Keq. Concentration changes never change Keq, only temperature does.
scenes.secondary: Trap 2: 'higher temperature means more energy so more product'. For an exothermic forward reaction, heating shifts it LEFT. Trap 3: assuming the added reactant is fully consumed. The system only partly counteracts, so the new equilibrium has more of both.
scenes.callout: Only T changes Keq; hotter isn't always righter; shifts are partial.
scenes.caption: Concentration never changes Keq; heat ≠ automatic right shift; counteraction is partial.

voiceover.text: Three traps. Trap one, thinking that adding more reactant increases the equilibrium constant. It does not. Concentration changes shift the position but never touch the constant, only temperature changes the equilibrium constant. The new equilibrium has different concentrations, but the same constant. Trap two, the lazy reasoning, higher temperature means more energy means more product. That's wrong for any exothermic forward reaction, where heating actually shifts the equilibrium left and reduces the yield. Never argue more energy equals more products, argue from the endothermic direction instead. Trap three, assuming the species you added gets completely used up. It doesn't. The system only partially counteracts your disturbance, so after the shift the new equilibrium has more reactant and more product than before. Le Chatelier minimises the change, it doesn't erase it.

scenes.heading: Quick check
scenes.question: CoCl₂·6H₂O(s) ⇌ CoCl₂(s) + 6H₂O(g) is pink on the left, blue on the right, forward endothermic. A chemist heats the pink paper in an oven. What colour does it turn, and why?
answerSteps: The forward reaction is endothermic, so heat behaves like a reactant.
Heating is like adding that reactant → shift right toward products.
The right side is anhydrous CoCl₂, which is blue.
Water is driven off as vapour, regenerating the dry blue form.
scenes.caption: Heating endothermic CoCl₂·6H₂O shifts right → blue (anhydrous).

voiceover.text: Quick check. Cobalt chloride hexahydrate decomposing to anhydrous cobalt chloride plus water vapour is pink on the left and blue on the right, and the forward reaction is endothermic. This is the colour change inside those little silica gel sachets. A chemist heats the pink paper in an oven. What colour does it turn, and why? Pause and reason it through. Right, let's check it. The forward reaction is endothermic, so heat behaves like a reactant, picture heat on the left. Heating is then like adding a reactant, so the system shifts right, toward products. The product side is anhydrous cobalt chloride, which is blue, and the water gets driven off as vapour. So the paper turns blue. That's literally how you regenerate a humidity indicator, pop it in the oven and the heat drives the equilibrium back to the dry blue form. Pink in the rain, blue in the desert.

scenes.heading: Lock it in
points: LCP: a disturbed equilibrium shifts to partially oppose the disturbance.
Add a species → shift away from it; remove a species → shift toward it.
Heating shifts toward the endothermic direction.
Concentration changes shift position but never change Keq.
Temperature is the only disturbance that changes Keq.
scenes.finalPrompt: Predicting a shift? State the direction AND justify it with LCP language.
scenes.caption: Five takeaways: LCP statement, concentration rule, temperature rule, Keq behaviour.

voiceover.text: Let's lock it in. Five things to take away. First, Le Chatelier's principle, a disturbed equilibrium shifts to partially oppose the disturbance. Second, the concentration rule, add a species and the system shifts away from it, remove a species and it shifts toward it. Third, heating always shifts the equilibrium in the endothermic direction. Fourth, concentration changes move the position but never change the equilibrium constant. And fifth, temperature is the only disturbance that actually changes the equilibrium constant. In the exam, never just say which way it shifts, always state the direction and justify it with Le Chatelier reasoning. See you next lesson.
